EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold winter storm moved down the California coast and into Southern Arizona.  The winter storm interacted with a significant amount of subtropical mositure producing widespread rain and significant mountain snow.  Flooding was also a problem across the Tucson Metro Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hannagan Meadow Lodge located at 9,160 feet in the White Mountains reported a storm total snowfall amount of 24 inches.
The post office located in Summerhaven on Mount Lemmon reported a storm total of 14 inches of snow and over an inch of rainfall on top of that.

After several days of rain and mountain snow several roads across Eastern Pima County were closed due to flooding.  These road closures included but were not limited to:  	The Canada Del Oro Wash at Overton Road and at La Cholla Road 	The Aqua Caliente Wash at Soldiers Trail Road, Limberlost and Fort Lowell	The Tanque Verde Wash at Tanque Verde Loop and Wentworth Road Rainfall amounts ranged from a quarter of an inch to an inch and a half in some higher elevation areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Hualapai Mtn Park reported a foot of new snow during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across northern Arizona from January 3rd to January 5th. Heavy rainfall fell at elevations below 6000 feet along and south of the Mogollon Rim. Some elevations between 5000 to 6000 feet saw heavy rainfall turn to snowfall during the event as snow levels lowered.  Some rainfall amounts (in inches) include: Cornville 2.44, Jerome 3.25, Pine (5500') 3.23, McNary 2.59, Cherry 2.69, Crown King 2.99, Humbug Creek 1.71, Iron Springs 1.71, and Payson 3.24.  A 12-inch water main in Prescott broke where it crossed the rain swollen Willow Creek. This affected about 1000 customers with low water pressure or no water at all. The break was isolated until the creek went down and repairs could be made. Two fatalities occurred on the Tonto as three young people attempted to cross the creek with a backhoe.  The backhoe tipped over and two of the people drowned.Rising water in the Little Colorado River north of Winslow cut off 16 homes in the Birdsprings Chapter. Residents in Pumpkin Center (35 miles southeast of Payson) were cut off by high water in Tonto Creek. The Big Cino Wash stranded 35 to 40 people in Paulden.

The final storm in a series of powerful winter storms to affect Arizona moved across the state from January 7th to the 11th with two significant periods of precipitation. This storm produced widespread rain...even at elevations above 7000 feet then snow at above 6000 feet with more rain at lower elevations.

A powerful winter storm began to move across northern Arizona on January 3rd and lingered into early January 5th. Widespread snowfall was reported above 5000 feet with generally 4 to 12 inches of total snowfall from 5000 to 6000 feet...12 to 20 inches from 5000 to 7000 feet...and 20 to 45 inches above 7000 feet. Heavy rainfall also occurred with the storm with elevations below 6000 feet along and south of the Mogollon Rim receiving generally one to two inches of rainfall. Some elevations between 5000 to 6000 feet saw heavy rainfall turn to snowfall during the event as snow levels lowered.Two teenagers week killed on January 3rd when their car slid on ice and hit a truck south of Hon Dah.Some storm snowfall totals (in inches) inclued: Alpine 7.5, Bellemont 25.4, Munds Park 18.5, Crown King 8.0, Payson 8.0, Flagstaff 35.0, Pine 10.0, Forest Lakes 37.0, Prescott 4-8, Grand Canyon North Rim 14.0, Grand Canyon South Rim 9.0, Greer 18.0 Hart Prairie 34.0, Heber 8.0, Williams 23.5, Jaacob Lake 12.0, Yarnell 7.0,  Pinetop 13, Fort Defiance (8000') 12, Arizona Snowbowl ski resort (10800') 47, Sunrise Ski resort 33,  Mormon Lake 25, Black Mesa 8-12, and Witeriver 8-10.For the city of Flagstaff...the storm total snowfall reached 35.0 inches during this storm...which made this event the 9th snowiest storm since weather records began in 1898. The 2-day total of 32.4 inches of snowfall was the 6th highest 2-day total since 1898. Record snowfall of 13.6 inches was recorded on January 3rd along with record snowfall of 18.8 inches on January 4th. The 18.8 inches of snow on January 4th was the 14th snowiest calendar day on record. 800 homes were without power in Walker (southeast of Prescott) when wet heavy snow broke the power line feeding into town.

A cold and moist winter storm moved into western Arizona. It brought heavy snowfall amounts to sections of Yavapai and western Coconino Counties. Ash Fork received 8 inches of snow by sunrise. As the storm moved into central Arizona it produced widespread snow over the Western Mogollon Rim. Snow accumulations of 4 to 10 inches were common across the region.

A low pressure system west of the Baja, Mexico Peninsula spread abundant subtropical moisture across the desert southwest. The result was widespread precipitation and river flooding. The Gila River at the Town of Duncan had moderate flooding from the large amount of precipitation that not only fell in Arizona but also in New Mexico. In the Town of Duncan smaller dikes broke allowing water to backup into the town.  Damage occurred to a residence near Duncan High School, and a trailer downstream of the high school.  Also, U.S. Highway 70 near the high school was covered with four feet of water and the approach ramps to the highway were overtopped with flowing water. East Avenue and low lying areas in the west end of the Town of Duncan were evacuated on the evening of Saturday February 12, 2005. The railroad tracks also on the west end of Duncan were covered with water and power went out in the west side of the town.  The Gila River at Duncan reached a maximum crest of 23.28 feet on February 13, 2005 at 0345 MST.   The San Francisco River at the Town of Clifton had minor flooding reported.  There was no damage reported in the Town of Clifton.  However, there was water to the bottom of the Railroad Bridge which stopped railroad traffic from the Morenci Mine and minor overflow of the river in the northern end of Clifton.  Also, the town gates, designed to divert water away from the Town of Clifton were closed isolating the town from road and railroad access from the north.  The San Francisco River at Clifton reported a peak crest of 23.49 feet on February 12, 2005 at 1230 MST.The Town of Solomon at the Gila River reported minor flooding.  The Solomon Road, Pima Road, and Thatcher Road bridge approaches were all flooded and closed.  U.S. Highway 70 Bridge near Bylas was also flooded and closed.  The Gila River near Solomon recorded a maximum crest of 19.63 feet on February 13, 2005 at 1430 MST.

After several days of rain, associated with a low pressure system, flooding occurred in Eastern Pima County.  Rainfall totals throughout the area ranged from 0.50 inch in the valleys to over 3 inches in the mountains.  The Tucson International Airport had a storm total rainfall amount of 0.85 inch and Mount Lemmon reported 3.34 inches over the 2 day period.  After all the rainfall several streets through the Tucson Metro Area were closed due to flooding.  The following roads were closed:	La Cholla Road at the Canada Del Oro Wash	Overton Road at the Canada Del Oro Wash	Limberlost Road at the Agua Caliente Wash	Soldiers Trail at the Agua Caliente Wash	Fort Lowell Road at the Agua Caliente Wash	Tanque Verde Loop at the Tanque Verde Wash	Wilds Road at the Canada Del Oro Wash	Loma Alta Road at Rincon CreekA 64-year-old woman was swift water rescued from her vehicle in Rincon Creek after her car got stuck in the high flood waters.  Also high rising water in Sabino Creek led officials to issue an advisory for residents near Sabino Canyon Creek.  No homes were flooded, but the rapid rising water was at bankfull in the creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock slide reported along Arizona Route 77 due to heavy rainfall and rapid runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system drew moist subtropical air from the Pacific to give northern Arizona widespread moderate to heavy rains. This precipitation event began Thursday night (02/10) and lasted through the early hours on Sunday (02/13). Rainfall totals of 2 to 3 inches were common in many locations...with locally heavier amounts found in portions of Yavapai and Northern Gila counties.Flooding caused road closures in Black Canyon City, Walker, Pinedale, and Globe. Paper Mill Road in Snowflake was washed out by the flood waters. Highway 377 was closed due to flooding between Heber and Holbrook. A trailer park in Black Canyon City was evacuated before the water rose into the parking lot. No trailers were damaged. Minor pasture flooding was reported in Cornville. A trailer park in the community of Tonto Creek was evacuated. Flood waters entered homes in Porter Creek Estates (near Show Low). Heavy rain caused street flooding in Black Canyon City. The heavy rain also caused a traffic accident on I-17 at mile marker 247.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Short-lived funnel cloud sighted near Maricopa, moving north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rains associated with a mid-winter storm system moved slowly across central and southern Arizona. Rainfall intensity increased significantly during the evening of February 11, and Alert rain gauges began to report excessive rainfall, exceeding 1.5 inches per hour during the early morning hours of February 12. Rural roads of north and northeastern Maricopa County became flooded by washes running heavy with the rain runoff.  The Hassayampa River eroded its banks near Wickenburg washing away two mobile homes and two vehicles.  The Jack Burden Road in Wickenburg was washed out as well as utility poles along the road were lost.  The Maricopa County Sheriff's Office was credited with rescuing 21 individuals in 11 separate flood water incidents during the weekend.  Rock and mud slides along highway 60 from Superior to Globe were reported by the afternoon of February 12.  Eventually, the Salt River and Verde River responded to the inflow, with Granite Reef Diversion Dam spilling 35, 345 cubic feet per second.  Down stream, the river bottom road crossings on the Salt, Verde and Gila rivers, to the Painted Rock Reservoir were flooded.  The Tempe Town Lake, located in the Salt River Channel, had to lower its inflatable dams to allow the normal flow of the Salt River to occur.  This storm system was the final significant 2004-2005 winter event that brought a season's total of about 9 inches to the three counties, with a few locations receiving much more, such as Carefree-Cave Creek that reported a 3 month total of 13.66 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A HAM operator in Chino Valley reported a funnel cloud.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure over southern Utah brought very strong winds to much of northern Arizona on  April 19. The strongest winds were observed from East Flagstaff, into the Little Colorado River Valley, and into the Winslow area.  The highest wind gusts were reported in Leupp (76 MPH), Doney Park (64 MPH), and Winslow (63 MPH). In Winslow, there were some power outages and a trampoline blew into a car. A semi truck and trailer was blown onto its side on I-40 near Winslow. The visibility was below 1/2 mile between Holbrook and Winslow between 4:00 and 5:00 PM.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved across northern Arizona on April 4 with very strong wind. The strongest wind was in the eastern Little Colorado River Valley. St Johns airport had a wind gust to 59 MPH. The Petrified Forest National Park reported sustained wind between 40 and 50 MPH with a peak wind gust of 68 MPH. Blowing dust reduced the visibility to near zero at the park.

A strong Pacific cold front brought high winds to the Kaibab Plateau overnight April 6 to 7. Remote Automated Weather Stations (RAWS) recorded peak wind gusts of 73 MPH at Four Springs and 60 MPH at Paria Point.
A funnel cloud was sighted east of Lake Mary Road in southeast Flagstaff.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house trailer was moved 30 to 40 feet and ended up on its side. No one in the trailer was hurt. Two power poles were also blown down. The wind lasted less than ten minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure brought showers and thunderstorms to much of northern Arizona on March 25th. Snow fell as low as 5500 feet. Snowfall was the greatest in favored locations of west facing exposures.  Storm total snowfall amounts included (in inches): Bellemont 8.2, Williams 7, Forest Lakes 9.0, Hart Prairie 10.0, and  Alpine 8.8.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Arizona Monday morning, March 15, bringing heavy snow and gusty north winds to the Mogollon Rim and Little Colorado River Valley. Snow continued to fall Monday evening over southern Navajo and Apache counties before ending by midnight. Storm total snowfall amounts as of 9 AM Tuesday, March 16, include (in inches): AZ Snowbowl 11-13, East Flagstaff 13, Meteor Crater 11, Flagstaff Pulliam Airport 9, Hart Prairie 9, Mormon Lake 8, Winslow 8, Bellemont 6.5, Williams 5,  Happy Jack 6, Blue Ridge 6-9, Holbrook 6, Forest Lakes 4, and Heber 4.The heavy snowfall, more than two inches an hour, hit Flagstaff just before the morning rush hour. This created havoc with traffic...more than 40 traffic accidents were reported in the Flagstaff area. Only three of the traffic accidents involved minor-injuries. There was an 11-mile backup on I-40's westbound lanes near Flagstaff.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds associated with thunderstorms damaged roofs and carports. Power was knocked out in parts of the Yuma Foothills area. Small hail was also reported with these thunderstorms.
Motorist on Interstate 10 observed funnel cloud.
A thunderstorm produced dime sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail in Fort Valley (10 miles northwest of Flagstaff).
A thunderstorm produced dime sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail in Heber-Overgaard.
A funnel cloud formed along a gust front in east Flagstaff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture moving into Arizona from northern Mexico helped to generate thunderstorms across portions of Southeast Arizona throughout a 5 day period towards the end of the month of May.  Severe storms developed along several boundaries that moved through Southeast Arizona throughout the 5 day time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The body of a 70-year-old man was found in a Cemetery in Tucson on Sunday, May 29, 2005.  The man was killed by lightning on Saturday night while visiting a gravesite at the cemetery.
An isolated thunderstorm near the City of Sierra Vista produced 0.75 inch hail.
A thunderstorm near the Sierra Vista area produced 0.75 inch hail.  A trained spotter reported that hail covered the ground.

Noon Creek overtopped highway 366 leaving debris on the roadway.  The flash flood resulted from one inch of rain that fell in 20 minutes at the Noon Creek ALERT rain gage. 1.61 inches of rain fell between 4:00 pm MST and 5:00 pm MST at Noon Creek.
21,400 customers in Cities of Nogales, Tubac, and Rio Rico lost power after lightning struck a power substation. The power was lost at about 6:45 pm MST and was restored again by 11:00 pm MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 3/4 inch diameter hail was received from about 6 miles northwest of Munds Park.
Two resorts just north of Sedona reported 3/4 inch to 1 inch diameter hail for about 5 minutes.
One inch diameter hail was reported at the South Rim of the Grand Canyon.
3/4 inch hail was reported at the Show Low Airport.
Canyon de Chelly reported less than 1/4 mile visibility in blowing dust for 15 minutes.

A tornado was observed by law enforcement and the general public from I-40 between mile post 308 and 311.  One report put the tornado on the ground about 20 miles east-northeast of the Petrified Forest National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered over the southern plains helped to maintain enough moisture across Southeast Arizona to prompt the development of showers and thunderstorms.  Although most storms stayed over higher terrain and were non-threatening, one thunderstorm that developed in Central Pima County caused wind damage and produced significant rainfall in the community of Queens Well (which is about 27 miles to the Northeast of the community of Sells).  The Queens Well rain gage reported 0.74 inch of rain in 25 minutes as the thunderstorm moved through the area.  Additionally, several residents reported wind damage to their property including; damage to a screen door, an uprooted tree, roofing damage on several homes, and damage to a ramada.  Based on the above damage reports the wind gusts was most likely not in the severe criteria and was estimated to be about 43 knots (49 mph).  The thunderstorm was shorted lived lasting only about 25 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense blowing dust reduced the visibility to about a quarter mile between Florence and Queen Creek.
A severe thunderstorm produced one inch hail (quarter size) that fell along Highway 191 between Hannagan Meadow and Alpine.
Law enforcement reported that dust, stirred up by strong winds, reduced visibility along Interstate 10 near Tangerine Road in the Town of Marana.
Cochise County Flood Control forwarded reports of flooding along Ramsey Road east of Highway 92 and west of Moson Road with a significant amount of debris over the road.
Strong winds, estimated to be 65 mph (56 knots) caused four power poles to be downed at Manville Road and Reservation Road.
A large tree was blown down by strong thunderstorm winds (estimated to be 60 mph) at the Tucson Mall on North Oracle Road in Tucson.
A strong thunderstorm on east side of the City of Tucson caused penny size hail (three quarter inch) at the Park Place Mall.
Strong thunderstorm winds, estimated to be about 60 mph (52 knots) downed a power pole on Wetmore Road near Oracle Road in Tucson.
A strong thunderstorm south of the City of Tucson and east of the City of Green Valley produced penny size hail (three quarter inch) in the Town of Corona De Tucson.

A tightly formed dust devil was picked up into a rapidly developing updraft of a thunderstorm overhead.  The column stretched, narrowed, and strengthened before dissipating about 1.5 minutes after formation. There was no damage and no injuries.

Numerous reports were received from trained spotters regarding very heavy rainfall with amounts of 1 to 2 inches falling in less than 45 minutes. There were reports of 6 to 8 inches of water flowing through streets and yards in Sierra Vista and Hereford.

An off duty National Weather Service employee reported that there was 1 to 2 feet of water covering 1st Avenue at Roger Road. Several other locations along 1st Avenue were also flooded, and portions of 1st Avenue were impassable.
A trained spotter reported that two feet of water covered Houghton Road south of Interstate 10.  The road was impassable and closed due to the flooding.
The Incident Meteorologist on the Florida Fire in Madera Canyon (10.5 miles east of Amado) reported that there was flash flooding in Madera Canyon with debris across Madera Canyon Road.
Streets flooded due to heavy rainfall in the Rita Ranch subdivision on the southeast side of Tucson.
Multiple reports of street flooding in the Town of Tubac and the Town of Rio Rico.  Spotters reported rainfall amounts of 1.60 to 2.11 inches in a short period of time.
"Heavy" flooding in Golden Valley.
6" diameter tree limbs blown down.
At Hualapai Peak.
The Hannagan Meadow Lodge in the Town of Hannagan Meadow, reported 0.88 inch hail falling and covering the ground.
Three quarter inch hail was reported by a trained spotter in the Town of Benson.
A 20 foot tall, 8-inch diameter tree was blown down by a thunderstorm wind gust on Avra Valley Road, 1/2 mile west of Clayton Road in the Town of Marana.
Tree limbs down and buildings damaged.
Lightning caused a fire at a North Peoria home, completely destroying it.
Strong thunderstorm wind gusts snapped one power pole in half which then caused seven others to topple down 17 miles north of the Town of Willcox.
Trained spotters reported three quarter inch hail and nickle size hail with a severe thunderstorm that moved across the Tucson Metro Area.

A severe thunderstorm over the Tucson Metro Area caused damage in several areas, including a power pole snapping near North Pantano Road and East Speedway Boulevard, leaving 10,000 customers without power. Additionally, power lines were downed at the intersection of Oracle Road and Wetmore Road.

Thunderstorm winds associated with a severe thunderstorm caused a large Eucalyptus tree to be blown over onto a home causing severe roof damage to that home. Another home nearby which was under construction also had wind damage.
Avra Valley Road was closed due to flooding.
Heavy rain over Mount Graham caused flooding and closure of several roads including: Aravapia Road, Bookerson Road, and Ash Creek Road.

Two to four inches of rainfall near the Village of Sells, caused several of the washes in the area to flood. A vehicle entered into the Artesa wash, which at the time was running very high with water, and became stuck.  Family members tried to get everyone out of the car and to safety but a one-year-old girl could not be rescued and was later found downstream.
1st Street between Stone Road and Main Street was flooded and closed with 6 to 10 inches of water running across the road in some places.
Heavy rain across the Tucson Metro Area caused several streets to be flooded and closed. The Stone Avenue underpass was closed due to several feet of standing water.
The Town of Marana Police reported that there were several streets closed due to flooding. These streets included but were not limited to Tangerine Road and Twin Peaks Road.

This was the third warmest July on record in Phoenix. The average monthly temperature was 97.3 degrees, just 4 tenths of a degree shy of the record warmest July which was set in 2003. The average maximum temperature for the month was 109.5 compared to a normal of 106.6 degrees. Several daily high temperature records were tied or broken, including 115 degrees on July 12, 114 degrees on July 13, and 116 degrees on July 17, the hottest day of the month.The average minimum temperature for July was 85.0 degrees, compared to a normal of 82.9. The daily low temperature records that were tied or broken included July 16, 17, 18, 19 and 21st. The low temperature on July 17 was 93, just 3 degrees shy of the hottest minimum ever in Phoenix. The onset of the wet season, usually around the 7th, was delayed until the 17th.  Even after July 17, there was not much humidity, and the total rainfall for the month at Phoenix Sky Harbor airport was only .16 inches.  Authorities have attributed 30 deaths to heat, nearly all victims were homeless.
Winds took down tree branches and damaged a car near Hayden and Thomas Roads.

Power lines down in south Phoenix. At the height of the storm, more than 40,000 APS customers were without power. The Phoenix Fire Department responded to 200 calls for service. Two large trees were uprooted at the Wigwam Resort and Golf Club.
House fire reported at 11620 N 114th Drive. At least 4 palm tree fires were reportedly started by lightning strikes.
Tree down.

Very heavy rainfall, about 2 inches per hour, caused flooding of low spots and washes. The peak flow in Hartman Wash, was reported as 1200 cfs. Major damage occurred at Bear Cat Manufacturing where a large robotic welding building was destroyed by the flood.
Several power poles down along US route 60.
Law enforcement reported that there were streets flooded near the Village of Gu Achi (Santa Rosa) in Central Pima County. This included flooding near milepost 40 on Federal Road 34.

After significant rainfall across the southeast side of the Tucson Metro Area several roads were impassable and closed due to high water. Rainfall amounts included 1.03 inches at Pantano Wash and Houghton Road, and several reports of an inch to an inch and a half from spotters.  Several roads were closed including: Valencia Road, Kolb Road, Pantano Road, and Escalante Road.

Pima County Flood Control reported that Overton Road at the Canada Del Oro Wash was closed due to flood waters. The road remained closed into the overnight period. Earlier that afternoon three rain gages in the middle of the upper Canada Del Oro watershed reported rainfall amounts in excess of 1.50 inches within an hour.

United States Border Patrol agents from the Naco Border Patrol Station rescued a woman who was trapped in her vehicle in a flooded wash. The woman was trapped by the flood for more than 45 minutes before the officials were notified. When rescuers did arrive the car was surrounded by rising, swiftly running water that was about 2 inches from the top of the car door and above the victims waist inside.

Flash flooding caused several roads to flood on the Southwest side of the Tucson Metro area. This flooding caused several road closures including:Camino Verde Road at Valencia RoadCamino de La Tierra Road at Valencia RoadValencia Road and Ajo HighwayWestover Road from Valencia Road to Drexel RoadMission Road from Valencia Road to Drexel RoadDue to the flooded roadways there were also four swift water rescues performed during this flash flood event. The Drexel Heights Fire Department pulled two cars out of the intersection of South Camino Verde Road and West Valencia Road; a sport utility vehicle out of the flood waters at South Camino Verde Road at West Ajo Way; and a car with a man and child inside out at South Camino Verde Road and West Arrow Road. There were no injuries.
Pima County Flood Control reported that there was flooding along the Pantano wash and that Harrison Road was closed due to the flooding.
Trees down and shingles blown off roofs. Near Greenfield and Broadway roads in Mesa, 1.75 inches of rain was reported. Power was out for 600 SRP customers in Chandler.
About 41 power poles blown down by strong winds along route 85 between Buckeye and Gila Bend.
Several homes destroyed or damaged due to winds. One mobile home was flipped over several times with the resident suffering multiple injuries.

Power lines down, trees uprooted, and shingles blown off roofs across a large portion of northwest Phoenix. At least 30 trees were downed by winds at the Palm Ridge Rrecreation Center in Sun City West. One automatic weather station at the White Tank mountains measured the 60 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure across Arizona caused extreme heat in the desert Southwest with temperatures above seasonal normals.  The extreme heat lasted for the first two weeks of July with some of the hottest days being over the Fourth of July weekend. Throughout this heat wave several bodies, some identified and some not, where found in the desert, all were believed to have died as a result of the extreme heat.Below is a table of the temperatures for several stations across Southeast Arizona during the Fourth of July weekend:		TUS	DUG	OLS	Sells	O.P. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous homes damaged, and trees and power lines blown down.
Thunderstorm winds caused numerous large tree limbs (6 inches in diameter) to be downed on the roadway near Harrison Road and Catalina Highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes knocked off their foundations in Fort Mohave and Mohave Valley.
A thunderstorm on the Northeast Side of the Tucson Metro Area caused high winds, up to 58 mph, which blew a large tree over at the intersection of Swan Road and Sunrise Road.
A trained spotter near the City of Green Valley reported that a tree had been uprooted by strong winds at the intersection of La Canada Road and Casa Verde Road.
The Bisbee Police Department reported three quarter inch hail.
A trained weather spotter in the Town of Douglas reported nickle size hail.
Occupants of a stranded vehicle were rescued at 128th Ave and Galaxy. Spotters reported up to 1 inch of rain in just 45 minutes.
About 34 power poles down near County Road 13 leaving 1,000 customers without power.
Power lines and trees down near I-17 and Glendale. Winds reached 53 mph in Fountain Hills and caused areas of blowing dust across Mesa and Tempe.  As many as 8 boats were capsized on Tempe Town Lake.
Power poles down at Ave 45E and County 5th.
Near zero visibility due to dense blowing dust possibly related to an accident on I-8 near Dateland.
Thunderstorm associated winds blew down three power poles near Grant Road and Interstate 10 in Tucson.
Flooding also reported in Desert Hills and Mohave Valley.

A severe thunderstorms in the Town of Benson produced strong winds (estimated to be 60 mph) and caused several power poles to be downed resulting in widespread power outages throughout the Town of Benson.
Cochise County Flood Control reported that Ramsey Road was closed east of Highway 92 and Moson Road was also closed due to flooding over the roadways making them impassable.
Law Enforcement reported that there were flooding problems in Benson, mainly road flooding with some roads being impassable.
A strong thunderstorm near the Douglas / Bisbee International Airport caused a  68 mph ( 59 knot) wind gust which was observed by the ASOS as this location.

A thunderstorm that moved through the Southeast side of the Tucson Metro Area caused some damage as estimated wind gusts of 50 mph (43 knots) moved across the area. The winds blew a power line off its pole as well as blew a street sign down.
Three quarter inch hail was reported by the Bureau of Indian Affairs in Point of Pines.

Strong thunderstorm winds (most likely a microburst) caused severe damage to several trailer homes and several vehicles in the Town of Marana near Avra Valley Road. Three trailers had damage, while three others were completely destroyed, including one being thrown onto a vehicle, and another being wrapped around a telephone phone. There were no injuries.
Three quarter inch hail covered the ground at Kitt Peak Observatory.
Two large tree branches broke off of an Eucalyptus tree due to strong winds (estimated 50 mph)  in the Picture Rocks area.
Trained spotter reported three quarter inch hail near the Tucson Mountains, to the west of the City of Tucson.
Broadcast media showed footage of flooded streets and washes in the Town of Picture Rocks due to heavy rainfall in a short period of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over the southern portion of Eastern Pima County caused a 61 mph (53 knot) wind gusts. This wind gust was measured by the Incident Meteorologists for the Florida Fire, whose base camp was located at Continental.
Washes overflowed their banks.
Water into the first floor of some buildings in both Chloride and Dolan Springs.
Flooding at the intersection of Highways 68 and 93, and at mile marker 66 on Interstate 40.
2.5 feet of water over roads. One driver rescued.
A severe thunderstorm that moved across the City of Sierra Vista caused three quarter inch hail as well as numerous reports of pea size hail.
Strong thunderstorm winds near the Town of Patagonia caused a several hundred pound storage shed to be damaged when blown across a yard.  Also shingles were torn off several roofs in a neighborhood.
Three quarter inch hail was reported by a trained spotter near the Town of Mescal.

An outflow boundary from storms earlier in the evening moved through the Tucson Metro Area causing high winds including a gust of 82 mph (70 knots) on the roof of the University of Arizona Atmospheric Sciences building (3 story building).  Also, a roof was torn off a house.  An off duty National Weather Service employee estimated wind speeds to be 60 mph at 2000 MST.
A large tree was downed by strong thunderstorm winds onto the roadway south of River Road on 1st Avenue.  Also large tree limbs fell onto the roadway along 1st Avenue for several blocks.

Strong thunderstorm winds caused power poles and power lines to be downed at the intersection of 1st Avenue and Limberlost Road.  Also a large tree was blown down causing damage to an apartment complex.
Dime size hail.
Washes overflowing with large rocks and debris over many roads.
Light poles blown down in Mohave Valley.
A severe thunderstorm near the City of Sierra Vista produced three quarter inch hail and heavy rainfall.

A severe thunderstorms rolled across the southeast side of the City of Tucson causing several power poles to be downed, trees to be blown down, and an estimated wind gusts of 75 mph (65 knots).  Three of the power poles were along South Wilmot Road.
Water over the access road to Willow Beach.
Broadcast media reported several calls of quarter size (one inch) hail between the Town of Vail and the Town of Corona De Tucson.
Streets flooded.
Heavy rain over far Western Pima County caused flash flooding south of the Town of Ajo.  The flooding occurred along State Route 85 from milepost 60 to 80, which had to be closed.
Heavy rain over the Catalina Mountains caused flash flooding and a mudslide closing the Catalina Highway.
Several roads were closed in the northeastern portion of the Tucson Metro Area due to flooding.
The Department of Highways reported that the Stone Avenue underpass, in the Tucson Metro Area, was closed due to flooding from heavy rain in the area.

Several roads were closed due to flooding across the central and western portions of the Tucson Metro Area due to heavy rain. The University of Arizona campus recorded 1.61 inches and the National Weather Service office recorded 1.48 inches.

Heavy rain across the Tucson Metro Area caused the flooding of several roads throughout the city. Road closures included but were not limited to:Wilmot Road from Sahuarita Road to Andrada RoadOld Vail Connection east of Old Nogales HighwaySummit Road east of Old Nogales HighwayCamino Verde Road from Bilby Road to Valencia RoadValencia Road from Wade Road to Ajo HighwaySynder Hill Road from Sandario Road to 2 miles west of San Joaquin RoadOld Ajo Highway from San Joaquin Road to Ajo Highway

A mudslide and flooding event occurred in Madera Canyon after significant rainfall. This area is flood prone due to the fire that occurred only a month before. Large boulders and trees were moved with the mud and water down the mountain, doing damage to one residence.  No one was injured.
Quarter to Golf ball sized (1 to 1 3/4 inch diameter) hail fell in Cordes Junction.

Several power poles snapped as microburst winds struck near Extension Road and west Eighth Avenue. The downed power lines created temporary chaos as children were not allowed to leave three schools, and vehicles were not allowed into the area due to the live wires. About 4,000 people were left without power because of the storm.
Large hail reported near Higley and Broadway.
Gusty winds from strong thunderstorms caused a dust storm to over-spread the Phoenix metropolitan area and reduce visibility to near zero as reported by the Arizona Department of Public Safety.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St Johns Airport recorded a 62 MPH wind gust.
The Inner Loop Road in the City of Willcox was closed due to flooding.

Flash flooding began along the East Verde River near Whispering Pines around 415 PM MST. The river came up five feet  and flowed over the bridges on Houston Mesa Road. Big logs were seen floating down the river.
Heavy rain cause flooding in one house in Cordes Junction.
3/4 inch diamiter hail was reported in Cameron.
Penny sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail was reported at the Wupatki National Monument.
Quarter (1 inch diameter) to golf ball (1 3/4 inch diameter) sized hail fell in Camp Verde for up to 10 minutes.
Dime sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail was reported by a Department of Public Safety officer about 10 miles west of Cameron along State Route 64.
3/4 inch diameter hail was reported in Camp Verde.
Penny to quarter sized (3/4 to 1 inch diameter) hail was reported in Heber-Overgaard.
Heavy rain caused flooding on Highway 160 between Tuba City and Highway 89. The road between Tuba City and the Tuba City Airport also flooded. Both roads were closed.
A car was stuck in an overflowing wash near Beaver Creek School in Rimrock.
Nickel sized (7/8 inch diameter) hail was reported in Prescott Valley.
Dime sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail was reported in Ashfork.
Carport awning damaged.
Very heavy rainfall left a part of Yuma Foothills streets flooded.
Utility power poles blown down.
Utility power poles and trees blown down.
Trees and utility power poles were blown down by storm winds. Arizona Public Service Company reported 30 power poles were blown down.
Torrential rains up to 1/2 inch in 15 minutes, and one inch in an hour, caused washes in the vicinity of the community to over-flow and flood roads.
Penny sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail was reported in Lakeside.
3/4 to 1 1/3 inch diameter hail was reported on the west side of Flagstaff by two NWS employees on their way to work.
Heavy rain caused washes to flow along and over Highway 97 near Bagdad. Water and debris were still flowing across the road into the early afternoon.
Quarter to golf ball sized (1 to 1 3/4 inch diameter) hail fell in Seligman.
Some trees were knocked down by thunderstorm winds. One tree was at least 50 feet tall.
3/4 inch diameter hail fell in Meteor City.
Nickel to quarter sized (7/8 to 1 inch diameter) hail fell in Humbolt for at least 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor street flooding and debris on roadways in west Flagstaff.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Cave Creek Complex burned (east of Bumble Bee) area caused flooding downstream. A river gage in Maricopa County (in WFO Phoenix's CWA) showed more than an eight foot rise in New River.
Quarter sized (1 inch diameter) hail fell at the Winslow Police Department dispatch office.
3/4 inch hail with heavy rain was reported in Heber-Overgaard.
Thunderstorm winds caused some building damage and downed trees in Pinetop-Lakeside.
Dime size hail.
Lots of one inch diameter hail. Larger hail (1 1/2 inch diameter) caused damage to vehicles.
Thunderstorms with heavy rain and damaging winds moved east across the northern portion of the county.  Winds blew down 6 utility power poles.
Trees and utility power poles blown down.
A meteorologist with Davis Monthan Air Force Base relayed a report from the Town of Vail of three quarter inch hail.
A trained weather spotter in the Town of Oro Valley reported three quarter inch hail.
Several trained spotters in Cochise County reported 1 inch diameter hail covering the ground.
Heavy rain over Central Pima County caused Route 19 to be closed from milepost 10 to 20. Additionally, a vehicle was stuck in a wash along Route 19.

A trained spotter east of the Town of Patagonia reported that Cimmarron Road was impassable due to flood waters. The same spotter reported that they had received two inches of rain in less than one hour.
The Benson Police Department reported numerous streets closed due to flooding including Highway 80 near Valley Park Trailer Park. Residents had to be evacuated from 2 or 3 homes due to flooding.
Harrison Road at the Pantano Wash was closed due to flooding.
A county official for Central Pima County reported near golf ball size hail (1.75 inches) at the Village of South Komelick along Indian Route 19, 5 miles north of Coulic.

Heavy rains during the afternoon flooded highways and roads. A few business buildings and residential homes were damaged by flash flood waters.  An off-duty National Weather Service employee reported that a two hour rainfall of 3.18 inches occurred prior to 17:30 MST.

Rural Metro Fire Department rescued two motorists from vehicles trapped in flash flood waters in northern Pinal County.  Roads were closed, including Hunt Highway between Arizona Farms and Bella Vista Road; and the Hewitt Station Road between Queen Valley road and US Highway 60.

Strong thunderstorms over east Phoenix metropolitan valley caused lightning which struck up to 13 homes in a Mesa neighborhood. Dramatic damage occurred as a result of the lightning; windows were blown out of the houses, drywall was damaged, electric power service meters and circuit breakers were destroyed, electric transformers were blown out of the ground.
Utility power lines downed by strong winds.
DPS reported flooding on route 85.
Vicksburg Road closed from I-10 to route 60 due to flooding. Street flooding reported in Quartzsite.

Heavy rains from widespread thunderstorms caused flash flood waters to over-flow washes from New River east to the Seven Springs area and Camp Creek.  Rainfall runoff was higher than normal in the Cave Creek Complex area and contributed significantly to the rapid flooding. The first fatality involved a pickup truck with a horse trailer; the driver attempted to drive on a flooded road and the vehicle was swept away drowning the driver. The second fatality involved a seven year old girl who was being evacuated from a home along Camp Creek. The rescuer and the young girl attempted to cross the flooded creek on foot where the girl slipped from the grasp of the adult and was swept away and drowned.

Many roads closed in area due to rapid flooding from strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall.  Local rain gauge networks indicated that a rainfall between 3 and 4 1/2 inches fell during the late afternoon and early evening.
Strong thunderstorms caused lightning to strike an electric power line, resulting in a 3 hour disruption of service to customers.
Trained spotter in Gila Bend reported a rainfall of 1/2 inch in 30 minutes.  An automatic rain gauge at Hassayampa reported 1.38 inches of rain in one hour.
As many as 12 electric utility power poles were blown down by severe thunderstorm winds.  The storm winds also damaged the roof of the Paloma school building, and toppled a large tree onto a house.
Amateur radio operators reported trees and power poles blown down in Casa Grande.

One of the heaviest rainfall events of the 2005 season struck the greater Phoenix metropolitan.  Almost 3 inches of rain fell at many locations in the metro, causing roofs to collapse and streets to flood quickly.  Up to 120 residents at the Crystal Creek Apartments in Phoenix were evacuated after 83 apartment units were damaged by flood waters.  Additional roof damage was reported at the Scottsdale Community College, and Osco Drug store in Mesa, and a Frys grocery store in Tempe.
Awnings ripped off, and trees flattened by strong winds, very heavy rain and small hail.
Dense blowing dust reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile. A 14-car pileup was reported near Route 287 and Nafzinger Road, on the north side of Coolidge.
Near Lindsay and Riggs roads.
Seven power poles blown down near US 60 and Ironwood Drive.
One hour rainfall of .99 inch near Mesa Drive and McKellips. In less than an hour .98 inch was recorded by a gauge at Williams Field Road in East Mesa.
Motorist trapped on Old Web Road between Peppersauce and Alder washes due to flooding. All roads intersecting those washes were closed.
One inch diameter hail was reported in Payson. One and a half inches of rain also fell during the same thunderstorm.
Power poles down.
Roads damaged by heavy rains.
Dense blowing dust reduced the visibility to less than 1/4 mile.
Dilkon Police reported a funnel cloud in the vicinity of Dilkon.
Heavy rain (up to 5 inches) caused flooding along Townsend-Winona Road. One home had two inches of water in it.
Law Enforcement in Cordes Junction reported wind gusts to 60 MPH.
A spotter in Sedona reported 1.46 inches of rain in 45 minutes. The power was knocked out.
A pilot reported seeing a funnel cloud 5 miles north of Prescott.
Carrizo Creek flooded over Highway 191 about nine miles north of St Johns.

Heavy rain caused the closure of Highway 260 in the Pinetop-Lakeside area. Several culverts burst. Several homes had water damage. Highway 73 from Lakeside to Whiteriver was closed twice because of water flowing over the road. Up to 5 inches of rain was reported at the Pinetop Country Club. A spotter two miles north of McNary reported just over 4 inches of rain during the afternoon.

Heavy rain caused flooding along I-40 from Sanders to Lupton (from mile post 342 to the New Mexico state line).  Both east and west bound lanes were closed. A bridge on I-40 was under water near Lupton. Flooding was also reported northward to Window Rock where the bridge over Narrow Wash washed out and stranded 15 to 20 people.  Water also flowed over a bridge near the Window Rock Airport.
An off duty NWS employee reported a funnel cloud about 10 miles northwest of the Flagstaff Airport.
Heavy rain caused a rock and debris flow to cross Highway 93 near milepost 156 in southwest Yavapai County. A car hydroplaned in two inches of water on Highway 93 and crashed into a ravine.
Nickel to quarter sized (7/8 to 1 inch diameter) hail fell off and on for at lest 26 minutes in Prescott Valley.
Winds briefly peaked at 77 mph as microburst struck the airport. Windows were damaged at terminal 4.
Dozens of trees were blown down, with many cars and homes sustaining damages due to the winds. Most of the damage was in the Springfield Resort Community near Riggs and McQueen roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cave Creek Complex Wildland Fire began as three lightning caused fires on June 21st.  One fire started in Yavapai County and two fires started south of the county line.  One of those fires was put out.  The other fire, Cave Creek Fire, rapidly burned into Yavapai County.  These two fires later were joined through burn-out operations to become the Cave Creek Complex.  The fire was in rough and broken terrain with steep slopes. The vegetation types were oak-chaparral, brush, and grass. A wet winter contributed to abundant grass that had become tinder dry.  By the time the fire was controlled, it burned 243,950 acres, cost almost 14 million dollars, and involved 1320 fire fighting personal.  About 85 percent of those figures were related to the area in Yavapai County.  Two Type I Incident Management teams were assigned to this fire.  The fire was contained on July 8th at 1800 MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall across the Tucson Metro Area caused several roads to be flooded requiring several swift water rescues. A few of the roads that were closed due to flooding included but were not limited to:Wilmot Road from Sahuarita Road to Andrada RoadOld Vail Connection east of Old Nogales HighwaySummit Road east of Old Nogales HighwayOld Ajo Highway from San Joaquin Road to Ajo HighwayOverton Road at the Canada Del Oro WashThree swift water rescues were performed throughout the Tucson Metro Area which included rescues at the following locations:The Rillito Wash near the Tucson MallSt. Mary's Road near Interstate 10

Heavy rain caused State Route 80 near the City of Douglas to be flooded and impassable in three locations.  There was one swift water rescue performed.  The Bay Acres Mobile Home Subdivision was also flooded causing damage to some homes.
The Clifton Police Department reported that Ward Canyon Road in Ward Canyon flooded with 4 to 5 feet of water covering the roadway.
Heavy rain caused flooded roadways which led to a swift water rescue in a flooded wash near the Village of Sells.  There were no injuries.
The Town of Cochise had several roads flooded and intersections impassable due to flooding.
Several low water crossings were closed due to flooding in the Sahuarita area along Gunnery Range Wash and in nearby washes.
An off duty NWS employee reported that several large trees had been uprooted and broken at their bases by strong thunderstorm winds. Also two structures had shingles taken off their roofs.
A severe thunderstorm which moved across the Tucson Metro Area caused several large trees and power lines to be downed across the city.  Also there were multiple reports of roof damage.
The Cooperative Observer at Hannagan Meadow Lodge in the Town of Hannagan Meadow reported that quarter size (one inch) hail was covering the ground and still falling.
Strong thunderstorm wind gusts (possibly a microburst) knocked down 15 power poles along Grant Road, one woman was caught in her vehicle with energized wires on top of it.
A strong thunderstorm caused a large fence to be blown down.

Strong winds associated with a thunderstorm in the City of Safford, caused damage to two large observation towers, several trailers, and several vehicles as sheet metal was blown off a roof hitting the cars.
A trained spotter near the City of Green Valley reported three quarter inch hail from a severe thunderstorm.

After over an inch of rain fell across a large portion of the Tucson Metro Area, some locations with more than two inches, several roads became flooded, closed, and impassable. In addition to all the flooded roadways, several trailer homes located in the southern portion of the Tucson Metro Area, were flooded and surrounded by rising water.  Rescue teams evacuated several people from these homes. Brawley wash was out of its banks and flooding roadways causing them to be impassable.   Below is a list of some, but not all, of the roads that were closed due to flooding:Kinney Road from McCain Loop Road to north of the Desert MuseumPump Station Road from Avra Valley Road to Silverbell RoadEl Trio Road from Cocio Road to Pump Station RoadMission Road between Valencia Road and Drexel RoadOld Ajo Highway from San Joaquin Road to Ajo HighwayOverton Road at the Canada Del Oro WashBonney Avenue north of Benson HighwayAndrada Road at Wentworth RoadWilmot Road from Sahuarita Road to Andrada RoadFort Lowell Road at the Agua Caliente WashSnyder Hill Road between Sandario Road and San Joaquin RoadSoliders Trail at the Agua Caliente WashLimberlost Road at the Agua Caliente WashAvra Valley Road at Trico Road

Due to heavy rainfall the Santa Cruz River, which runs through the Tucson Metro Area, went over bankfull in a couple of locations. The Santa Cruz River at Congress Street (which is in Downtown Tucson) flooded walking paths along the river, washing some of them away.  As the water continued to move downstream it went over bankfull again South of Ina Road near Camino Del Cerro Road.
Two miles south of the Town of Amado a burm failed and caused two to three feet of water to run through a local farm, causing damage to crops and equipment in the field.
Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches across a portion of Cochise County caused several roads to be flooded and impassable.

Heavy rainfall across the southern portion of the Tucson Metro Area as well as in midtown, caused several roads to be flooded. A car was stuck in a wash under the Stone Avenue underpass, and the Stone Avenue underpass was then closed due to high flood waters.  Another car was stuck in a flooded roadway near Campbell Road  and Drexel Road.
Noon Creek overtopped Highway 366, the road was reported to be impassable due to flood waters and was closed. Also a rockslide was reported at the intersection of Noon Creek and Highway 366.

Widespread street flooding occurred in the City of Douglas. Water depth at Washington Avenue and Golf Course Road was reported to be 1.5 feet deep. State Highway 80, 2 miles northeast of Douglas, was closed due to flooding.  Two swift water rescues were performed.  2.50 inches of rainfall were reported within about 30 minutes in Douglas.

A swift water rescue was performed by the Tucson Fire Department on South Highland Avenue near the University of Arizona after a woman's car got stuck in the flooded roadway. The water was more than 2 feet deep when the woman attempted to drive through the flooded area. Rainfall amounts over three quarters of an inch and pea size hail were reported with this storm.
A trained spotter reported that Houghton Road between Interstate 10 and Sahuarita Road was closed due to flooding making the road impassable.
Heavy rain caused flooded roads in the Town of Klondyke including Klondyke Road, which was closed due to the flooding.

Heavy rain across the Tucson Metro Area caused several roads to be flooded and closed.  Wilmot Road between Andrada Road and Sahuarita Road was closed due to flooding.  There were four swift water rescues across the City of Tucson.
An observer in a fire lookout tower on Greens Peak reported a 76 MPH wind gust at 10,000 feet elevation.
Law enforcement in St Johns reported that a tornado was briefly sighted northeast of St Johns.
An Apache County Sheriff's Deputy reported seeing a tornado near Concho. It was possibly a "gustnado" along an outflow in that area.
Dime sized (3/4 inch diameter) hail fell in Star Valley (6 miles east of Payson) for 10 minutes.
A trained spotter saw a tornado about two miles north of Petrified Forest National Park.
The Sierra Vista Police Department reported widespread street flooding in the City of Sierra Vista.  Moson Road was closed due to flooding.
Santa Cruz Flood Control reported a foot of water over Madera Canyon Road at Milepost 12. There was debris and several 3 foot diameter boulders in the roadway.
A trained weather spotter near the City of Willcox reported water flowing over Highway 191 to the northwest of the Town of Sunsites and over Kansas Settlement Road.
Water flowing across the road on State Route 92 near milepost 341 caused a vehicle to roll over.

Heavy rain caused street flooding along Buffalo Solider Trailer and 7th Avenue near downtown Sierra Vista. A swift water rescue was performed on Calle Portal Road between Bluebrid Drive and Quail Run Road.

Road flooding in Town of Three Points caused a vehicle to become stuck in a flooded roadway, leading to a swift water rescue and the drowning of a 3 year-old little boy. Also, mud and water flooded a residential garage, and Brawley Wash flooded Mile Wide Road.
Washington Avenue and Highway 80 were closed due to flooding.
Power poles down.
A funnel cloud was reported south of Ash Fork.

A massive avalanche knocked down thousands of trees in Abineau Canyon and wiped out a 3/4 mile section of the Abineau Trail. The rare Arizona avalanche buried the trail under tons of trees, boulders and debris. Abineau Canyon is on the north side of the San Francisco Peaks within the Kachina Peaks Wilderness Area about 10 miles north of Flagstaff. The head of the canyon is over 12,000 feet above sea level and the toe of the avalanche was around 9,500 feet. Arizona Snowbowl, only 3 miles away, reported their second highest seasonal snowfall of 460 inches by the end of April.

One of those fires was put out.  The other fire, Cave Creek Fire, rapidly burned into Yavapai County.  These two fires later were joined through burn-out operations to become the Cave Creek Complex.  The fire was in rough and broken terrain with steep slopes. The vegetation types were oak-chaparral, brush, and grass. A wet winter contributed to abundant grass that had become tinder dry.  By the time the fire was controlled, it burned 243,950 acres, cost almost 14 million dollars, and involved 1320 fire fighting personal.  About 85 percent of those figures were related to the area in Yavapai County.  Two Type I Incident Management teams were assigned to this fire.  The fire was contained on July 8th at 1800 MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall over the burn area of the Two Bar Fire caused the Campaign Creek and other washes to flow. Streets were closed in Roosevelt Lakes and a vehicle got stuck in the Campaign Creek during the height of the flash flood.
The police department in the Town of Bisbee reported that several streets were flooded and impassable, as well as one home impacted by flood waters.
A car became stuck in the intersection of La Canada Road and Casa Verde Road in the City of Green Valley due to flooding.
A trained spotter in the City of Sierra Vista reported flooding of roadways.  Also, the Sierra Vista Police Department reported that Moson Road was closed due to flooding.
Law enforcement reported that Ramsey Road east of Highway 92 and west of Moson Road in the Town of Hereford was closed due to flood waters.
Tucson City Communications reported that Wilmot Road south of Interstate 10 was impassable and closed due to flood waters.

The Florida Fire was a lightning caused fire that started on July 7, 2005 in the Santa Rita Mountains about 25 miles south of the City of Tucson.  The fire burned a total of 23,183 acres, injured 13 firefighters and was contained on July 21, 2005 at 6 pm MST.  At the peak of the fire there were 986 personnel , and the total cost to suppress the fire was 8.1 million dollars.  Below is a few of the dates when main events occurred with this fire.July 9, 2005 - Some trails leading to the fire area are closed.July 11, 2005 - All public access to Madera Canyon Recreation area was restricted.July 13, 2005 - At 10 am MST Madera Canyon residents were evacuated.July 17, 2005 - Portions of the fire received a half inch of rainfall from thunderstorms.After the monsoon season got underway, rainfall helped aide the firefighters in putting the Florida fire out.

A thunderstorm producing strong winds moved through the southwest side of Tucson causing six power poles to be knocked down along Old Nogales Highway at Airport Road.  Over 4000 people were without power for over an hour due to the downed power poles.
A severe thunderstorm on the southeast side of Tucson produced 1.25 inch hail near the intersection of Golf Links Road and Harrison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado was sighted in a sparsely populated area about 40 miles east-northeast of Cameron near the intersection of Dinnebito Wash, Highway 264, and the Coconino/Navajo County lines. One ranch did sustain minor damage to the house, the hogan, and the sweat lodge. Several outbuildings were totally destroyed and/or carried around 100 meters from their original locations. A 55-gallon drum filled with grain was found empty a few hundred meters from its original location. There was considerable damage to the tree only a few feet from the house, while little damage to the house occurred. Some farm animals were lost (9 chickens and 2 cats). The storm continued to travel north-northeast into Navajo County through a sparsely populated area. A semi trailer was blown over on Highway 160 near the intersection of Route 564 and Navajo National Monument. There were several reports of funnel clouds and tornados in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported by law enforcement in Seligman.
A Department of Public Safety officer saw a tornado about 5 miles northeast of The Gap while driving on Highway 89.
Arizona Department of Public Safety reported 5 wrecks due to hail covered road on I-17 near Highway 69. There was one fatality in a wreck on Highway 69 between I-17 and Mayer.
One inch diameter hail was reported 5 miles east of Tuba City

Very heavy rainfall across the far north portion of the metropolitan area resulted in rapid runoff and flooding. The Seven Springs stream gauge indicated a sudden jump of the water level, from zero to 8.5 feet in only 20 minutes  The Camp Creek Alert gauge recorded a total of 3.11 inches, with 2.01 inches in one hour, ending at 5:15pm. Bartlett Road was washed out and impassable, trapping about 400 people as they were leaving Bartlett Lake. In Phoenix, one of the heaviest rains was reported at the East Fork of Cave Creek at 7th Avenue, with flooding of many streets in North Phoenix.
Truck blown off road.
High winds from nearby thunderstorms took down 16 power poles along highway 60, east of Gold Canyon.
Radar indicated a tornado in the Secret Canyon area 19 miles east-northeast of Perkinsville.  A later storm survey verified tornado damaged trees in this area.
